6.text: Which of the following has the most influence on the drag of a body?
options: The material characteristics of the body.

10.text: An object with a frontal area of 2m² exposed to an airflow of 30km/h produces a drag of 300N (approx. 30kp) at sea level. Under the same conditions, how much drag is produced by an object of similar shape but with a frontal area of 4m²?

11.text: An object with a frontal area of 2m² exposed to an airflow of 30km/h produces a drag of 300N (approx. 30kp) at sea level. Under the same conditions, how much drag is produced by an object of similar shape but with a frontal area of 1m²?

15.text: If the speed of the airflow to which a body is exposed doubles,
options: the drag will be multiplied by 4.
the drag will be multiplied by 16.
the drag will be multiplied by 8.

16.text: If the speed of the airflow to which a body is exposed is tripled,
options: the drag will be multiplied by 12.
the drag will be multiplied by 9.
the drag will be multiplied by 6.
the drag will be multiplied by 3.

17.text: If the speed of the airflow to which a body is exposed is quadrupled,
options: the drag will be multiplied by 4.
the drag will be multiplied by 8.
the drag will be multiplied by 16.

18.text: If the speed of the airflow to which a body is exposed is divided by 2,
options: the drag will be divided by 4.
the drag will be divided by 2.
the drag will be multiplied by 4.

19.text: An object with a frontal area of 2m² exposed to an airflow of 30km/h produces a drag of 300N (approx. 30kp) at sea level. What will the drag be of the same body in an airflow of 60km/h?

20.text: An object with a frontal area of 0.75m² exposed to an airflow of 30km/h produces a drag of 100N (approx. 10kp) at sea level. What will the drag be of the same body in an airflow of 90km/h?

21.text: An object with a frontal area of 2m² exposed to an airflow of 80km/h produces a drag of 1,200N (approx. 120kp) at sea level. What will the drag be of the same body in an airflow of 40km/h?

23.text: Which of the following has the most effect on drag on a body?
options: The difference between temperature and dew point.
The absolute humidity of the air.

24.text: If the density of the airflow to which a body is exposed doubles,
options: there is no change in the drag.

25.text: If the density of the airflow to which a body is exposed is halved,
options: there is no change in the drag.

26.text: If a body exposed to an airflow moves further away from the earth's surface,
options: this factor has no influence on its drag.
its drag will decrease just up to the troposphere and increase thereafter due to the change in temperature.

27.text: If a body exposed to an airflow moves further away from the earth's surface,
options: its drag diminishes faster in the lower layers of the atmosphere than in the higher layers.
its drag diminishes faster in the higher layers of the atmosphere than in the lower layers.
its drag diminishes in the lower layers of the atmosphere and increases in the higher layers.

28.text: A body exposed to an airflow at 4400m above sea level will have,
options: approx. 81% of the drag at sea level.
approx. 72% of the drag at sea level.
approx. 64% of the drag at sea level.
approx. 90% of the drag at sea level.

29.text: A body exposed to an airflow at 3300m above sea level will have
options: approx. 72% of the drag at sea level.
approx. 81% of the drag at sea level.
approx. 64% of the drag at sea level.
approx. 90% of the drag at sea level.

30.text: A body exposed to an airflow at 2200m above sea level will have
options: approx. 90% of the drag at sea level.
approx. 64% of the drag at sea level.
approx. 72% of the drag at sea level.
approx. 81% of the drag at sea level.

31.text: A body exposed to an airflow at 1100m above sea level will have
options: approx. 64% of the drag at sea level.
approx. 90% of the drag at sea level.
approx. 81% of the drag at sea level.
approx. 72% of the drag at sea level.

49.text: The term "angle of attack" represents the angle between

the chord of the profile and horizontal.
the upper and lower wing surfaces at the trailing edge.
horizontal and the relative air flow.

51.text: Which of the following profiles is exposed to an airflow with the largest angle of attack? 12

54.text: What is more or less the spread of lift over a wing which is exposed to an airflow with an angle of attack of + 10° ?
options: 2/3 on the upper surface, 1/3 on the lower surface.
1/3 on the upper surface, 2/3 on the lower surface.
1/2 on the upper surface, 1/2 on the lower surface.
1/4 on the upper surface, 3/4 on the lower surface.

55.text: Which of the following statements about the lift distribution is correct for a cambered wing flying an airflow with a positive angle of attack of 10° ?
options: 1/2 of the lift is created on the first third of the wing.
2/3 of the lift is created on the second third of the wing.
1/3 of the lift is created on the first third of the wing.
2/3 of the lift is created on the first third of the wing.

57.text: If the angle of attack of a paraglider flying at best glide is reduced by 2° from the angle for best glide then
options: the speed of flight increases initially and then stabilizes at the original value.
the speed of flight does not change.

58.text: If the angle of attack of a paraglider flying at best glide is reduced by 2° then
options: the lift coefficient (CL) initially increases momentarily before stabilizing at its original value.

59.text: If the angle of attack of a paraglider flying at best glide is increased by 2° then
options: the speed of flight increases initially before stabilizing at the original value.
the speed of flight does not change.

60.text: If the angle of attack of a paraglider flying at best glide is increased by 2° then
options: the lift coefficient (CL) initially increases momentarily before returning at the original value.

options: the point where the airflow is divided between the upper and lower wing surfaces.
the point of application of the resultant aerodynamic force.
the point where the airflow separates from the wing.
the point where the airflow on the wing upper surface rejoins the flow from the lower surface.

66.text: When a glider is flown with maximum load, the point of stall
options: occurs at the same speed and angle of attack independent of load.
occurs at a lower speed than with minimum load.
occurs at a higher speed than with minimum loading.

67.text: When a glider is flown with minimum load, the point of stall occurs
options: at the same speed and angle of attack independent of load.
at a lower speed than with maximum load.
at a higher speed than with maximum load.
at a smaller angle of attack.

69.text: If the positive angle of attack of a wing in an airflow is increased, the stagnation point moves
options: along the wing upper surface towards the trailing edge.
along the wing lower surface towards the leading edge.
along the wing lower surface towards the trailing edge.
along the wing upper surface towards the leading edge.

70.text: If the positive angle of attack of a wing in an airflow is decreased, the stagnation point
options: moves towards the leading edge on the wing upper surface.
moves towards the trailing edge on the wing lower surface.
moves towards the leading edge on the wing lower surface.

73.text: The term "average chord" is defined as
options: the average distance between the leading and trailing edges of a wing.
the difference in angle of attack of the wing cross-section at different points along the wing.
the distance between both wing tips.
the average loading per unit of wing surface area.

74.text: The term "wing loading" is defined as
options: the difference in angle of attack of the wing cross-section at different points along the wing.
the average distance between the leading and trailing edges of a wing.
the distance between the left and right wing tips.
the average loading per unit of wing surface area.

77.text: The aspect ratio is calculated in the following way
options: average chord x wing surface area.

78.text: Wings with a large aspect ratio have
options: a small span and a small average chord.
a large span and a small average chord
a large span and a large average chord.
a small span and a large average chord.

79.text: Wings with a small aspect ratio have
options: a large span and a large average chord.
a small span and a large average chord.
a small span and a small average chord.
a large span and a small average chord.

80.text: A wing's aspect ratio is 5. Which of the following statements is correct?
options: Its average chord is five times larger than its span
Its surface area is five times larger than its span
Its span is five times larger than its surface area
Its span is five times larger than its average chord

81.text: A wing's aspect ratio is 5. Which of the following statements is correct?
options: Its surface area is five times smaller than its span
Its average chord is five times smaller that its span
Its span is five times smaller than its average chord
Its span is five times smaller than its surface area

in the middle of the glider in front of the leading edge.

142.text: A hang glider pilot flies at 36 km/h (10 m/s) with a sink rate of 1 m/s. He reaches an area of sink where the air descends at 1 m/s. What happens if the glider maintains its speed?
options: The sink rate and the glide ratio decrease.
The sink rate and the glide ratio increase.
Its glide ratio will reduce from 10 to 5.
Its glide ratio will increase from 5 to 10.

The same in a rising or descending air current as in calm air.
